sentences.text: OK.
The things did not change immediately for us.
It was more of the larger cities in Holland that were immediately-- where-- where the Jews were maybe put in camps or anything.
But here we were all of 40 people and that included little kids, because, you know, we were little.
And so there were not that many people.
But my father made sure that he got the-- what should I say-- the order of the [NON-ENGLISH], which was the Dutch Jewish group.
And he thought that if all of us-- or at least the men of serving, let's say, military age-- that we all had a job.
They were all trained into doing work in the fields.
Because our little town was really a little town where they had-- they had a lot of farmers.
So we all knew how to grow things.
And we all learned things like that so that we could say we did not have to go to camps, because we were helping the farmers in our little community.
I was-- oh, I was already 20 years old.
Yeah.
I was-- I learned-- my mother, who was such a good seamstress, wanted me to learn to sew, because my sister's eyesight prohibited her to do so.
And I must say, I hated every minute of it.
I thought it was terrible to learn for me.
I did it because at that time, you didn't say no.
But I must-- I went to the next town by the name of Doetinchem.
I'll spell it for you.
D-O-E-T-I-N-C-H-E-M.
I think that's how it is spelled.
And there was a place, a store that had a-- let's say a [NON-ENGLISH] as it was called at the time, where they would make custom made dresses or whatever you needed.
The company originally-- I think the company was my father's cousin or something similar to that.
And I was sent there to learn how to sew.
I must say, I learned it real well.
It had-- everything had to do be done by hand.
In all my years, and all the clothing that I made ever since, I have never learned how to put a zipper in by-- using the sewing machine because I only learned to do it by hand.
And the lady who was the leader of the [NON-ENGLISH] was a German lady who had very good taste in everything.
And she really taught us very, very well.
No, I had already left there.
I-- I-- I don't remember-- no, I had already left there.
But I had to learned the basics and everything.
And I did meet a very nice girl there who later became my friend.
Who-- and then very many, many, many years later became our sponsor.
The lady lived in the German town of Emmerich, which was right across the border from me.
And she also worked in the same [NON-ENGLISH], as it was called.
And the two of us, we learned to sew.
And she came to our house.
And we would sew together.
My mother, of course, would help us.
And this particular lady, her name is Ruth.
Right now, she-- her name is Ruth Taub, but that-- this is her married name.
And she was one of the best designers in the city of New York for many, many, many years.
She is now retired, but I still see her on a yearly basis.
So that was a very, very nice-- and a friendship lasted all these years.
And so it was real nice.
Then after, I think, I couldn't-- oh, of course.
I couldn't travel by bus anymore.
That was in the beginning.
Oh, I remember now.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We all had to wear-- at that time, we all had to wear the yellow star, of course.
And we had to sit in particular-- there was a little-- there was a bus between our little town, 's-Heerenberg, and Doetinchem.
And-- everybody would, of course, look and see what you had.
And you had to-- later on, we were not allowed to go on the bus anymore.
So we had to do all kind of other things.
Everybody was living except-- everybody except for my older brother was living with my parents.
My younger brother would help my father with his work in the butcher shop.
My sister would cook and do the housework.
And my mother still would do her sewing, if possible.
But that's what I wanted to say to you.
There was very little money coming in.
Very, very little money.
So the-- our life changed completely.
There was hardly any money coming in.
I worked then.
I went to work in-- and stayed, let's say, would only come home once a week or sometimes once a month.
I would work in a place by the name of Dieren.
D-I-E-R-E-N, which is the first town where I was in hiding also.
I met some-- this was a store like-- what should I say?
Your remember Henshey's here in Santa Monica?
It was a store that sold clothing.
And I was a sales lady.
I was very, very young.
But I had a lot of fun with the people who worked there.
About-- there were six of us or seven of us.
And one of the people who was a salesperson there is Ben Fromm, who lives about 15 minutes away from me right here in Beverly [NON-ENGLISH], Los Angeles.
So he was one of the people.
He was a traveling salesperson, and I was in the store.
And we would sell ladies and gentleman's clothing.
But it was-- it got worse and worse and more difficult each and every time.
But nobody can--
Well, you had to give them everything that was silver or whatever.
Gold, silver, and whatever.
You had to.
Later on, I don't remember exactly, but we-- we ended up with very, very little, I should say.
I don't remember all the things that we hand to them.
But they would come and gather them.
And then we could not go to a store.
We had to-- we only could go to a store, I think, between 3:00 and 5:00.
Which if you have to go to, and the butcher, and the grocery, and the market.
There was no such a thing as a market.
You go-- you go to all these different stores, so you couldn't-- you did not have very, very much time to run the errands.
My father, fortunately enough for us, he usually ran our errands.
But it-- but it got-- I think we just took things as they came.
We-- well, we knew that we couldn't do anything about it.
At that time, we didn't really fear for our lives, because we were all trained in doing labor in the field, if it had to be.
So we thought with my father's-- what he would call an [GERMAN].
So the Germans-- you had like a passport given to him by-- not directly by the Germans, but through the Jewish community in Amsterdam, given to him that he was the head of the Jewish community.
And he had all the people working for the Germans.
So we were doing things for the Germans already.
We thought that would be sufficient.
The day that we were notified.
Oh, we did have things already.
We all had a backpack standing ready.
Each and everybody had a backpack standing there that in case we had to-- we would be transported anyplace.
Because there was already the transport took place at a time already in the largest cities.
But I think that we just thought, well, maybe we'll get away with it.
I do not know.
But we did have our backpacks ready.
And on February 11, I think it was, in 1943-- one of the other Jews, who also lived in the town, he actually risked his life by-- oh, yeah.
We had to be indoors at 8 o'clock.
There was a curfew.
And he came to our house.
I remember distinctly because I heard somebody come to the house.
And anybody coming to your house meant trouble, of course.
And we had a two story house, and I ran dowstairs, halfway downstairs on the steps to listen in on the conversation.
And there was a gentleman, a Jewish gentleman, who came to tell my father that we, the Straus family, was going to be put into jail and being transported.
We didn't know why, how, or whatever.
But that was the thing that he came to tell us.
And when he did get back to his own house again, he, at that same-- in that same evening, also fled his house.
And there was a conference in our living room, my parents sitting on the sofa.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Excuse me.
My father decided it was good for us to leave.
We did have people who would hide us.
And we did-- and he gave my brother-- I still remember him saying, you take care of the girls.
You go.
We had-- we had a forest not far away from us.
You take the girls to the forest, you stay there overnight.
And don't-- tomorrow morning, at 5 o'clock by the time it gets a little bit lighter, go to this farm where you can make a phone call, and then you can go to your hiding place.
We-- for us to get out of the house after 8 o'clock, after the curfew, the danger was just as-- you know, the danger was right there.
We could have been picked up by German soldiers.
Because at that time, we did have hundreds of German soldiers in what used to have been-- what used to be a-- what should I say?
A gathering place of-- well, not nuns, but what-- [NON-ENGLISH].
What do you call them?
Friars.
OK.
So they had taken those religious gentlemen out of there, and the Germans had taken that building.
So there were plenty of Germans around.
But we were very, very fortunate.
It took us about five, 10 minutes before we got to the ground of the castle that was in our little town.
And from there, since we of course knew-- we used to walk, and hike, and do everything.
We knew our way around so well, we were able to go through the forest and end up in a barn where we knew there was a wagon.
A horse and wagon.
A horse and buggy or like a buggy wagon, let's say.
And we stayed there.
We sat there during the night, the three of us.
It was February, so it wasn't really warm, but it was not that bad.
And early in the morning, at 5 o'clock in the morning, we then walked across the fields in like a little-- by a little-- well, you couldn't call it a mountain maybe.
Well, whatever you want to call it-- a little hike there.
And we went to this farm where we knew the people.
Where we could make the phone call in order for my friends in this town of Dieren, where I had worked, where Ben Fromm had worked also.
And we-- they would come and take us to their house or someplace in hiding.
We were maybe-- we had a very good house, home life.
But we were not kissing-- the kissing type.
So I think we-- I remember my mother sitting in her nightgown, and that's the one thing that I remember.
But my father said, come on.
Let's go and make sure.
And you do this and that.
So we would just-- we were just following instruction, and I don't think that we realized what was ahead of us.
I know that we didn't.
No.
No.
They went that night.
They were transported to the city of [NON-ENGLISH], which of course everybody knows.
And they were put in jail that night.
And I think two or three days later, they were put in a camp.
In the only camp-- concentration camp that was in Holland.
And I think they-- they went to Auschwitz within a week or something similar to that.
Very, very short time after that.
But we were--
Yes.
Our friends, Tima and Ali.
I'll just give you their first names.
I have it all described, and you can have the last names if you wanted to.
Shall I give you last names?
OK.
Ali van Gessel was her name, and Tima was her fiance.
And his name was Tima Beuving.
They were, at that time, not even engaged.
OK.
B-E-U-V-I-N-G.
And her name was Van, V-A-N, Gessel, G-E-S-S-E-L.
They came.
I am not 100%-- I do not know exactly whether they brought the extra bikes with them or that the farmer gave the bikes to us that we drove-- that we rode on.
My brother stayed behind.
He was going to go and stayed behind in the-- in the one place very close to where we ended up the second year, which is where we found him back.
We, the two girls, on the bicycle, we traveled about 25 kilometers.
So 15, 16 miles.
Somewhere around there, let's say.
In the dark.
And we still had our star on.
I never-- and we stopped someplace along the road, tore off the star, and buried it in a little ditch or something.

And we were able to-- I do not-- I don't remember exactly how dark it was, but we came to the river [NON-ENGLISH], which was quite a large river where Tima, our friend, had made sure there was a little rowboat.
A tiny little rowboat that got us across the [NON-ENGLISH].
Because the regular bridge where we normally would have gone over was occupied by the Germans, so that would not work.
So we had--
In the rowboat.
So there was just enough room for all of us and the little bikes.
No.
Harry stayed behind.
Only the two girls, my sister, and myself, and Tima, and Ali in this rowboat with the bicycles crossing the river.
The brother who was-- he took care of us up to this point until somebody else came to take care of us.
He knew that we would be in very good hands, so he had to make sure that he would find a place.
And he found a place.
He already knew where he was going to go, and he traveled over there, which was not that far.
Not as far as we had to go.
We came to the house of Ali van Gessel, who lived-- who was my friend all these-- or at least a couple of years.
And who had traveled with me.
You know, and we had become really good, good friends.
And her mother was a widow.
They were-- they were strict, very strict, Catholic people.
Beautiful people.
Unbelievable people.
I do not know whether you know.
We came to their house, and they-- we stayed with them for almost a year.
Almost a year.
Never asked us for anything as far as money or anything.
They took care of us from-- from the beginning to the end.
But the one thing that I want to mention is they-- they had seven children.
All in, there were-- this widow had seven children.
Ali was one of them.
I think Ali was the oldest girl.
And there were two girls who were nuns.
So I can assure you, they were very strictly religious people.
They would go to their church every morning.
You know, so the first thing they did, they started a-- a Catholic prayer for my parents.
It is called a novena.
It lasts seven days.
They prayed seven days for my-- for the well-being of my parents.
Un-- you know, that's things that you don't hear very often.
It was beautiful.
I was terribly mad.
My-- I was mad, mad.
I was-- my parents, who had been such good people.
It was unheard of.
I just gave up eating.
So if you want to know how come I didn't gain an ounce in 40 years or 50 years, that was one of the reasons.
I refused to eat.
I ate just enough to stay alive, and that was all I ate.
Although these people did everything to make us comfortable.
Everything.
[LAUGHS] My sister and I had a tiny little room.
It was also a two story house.
Everybody would go to work.
We were locked up in this-- in this little room.
And across the bed was a ironing board and a sewing machine.
And I would sew.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Repairing men's shirts, turning collars, and everything.
Repairing things.
And that's how we-- and the one thing, my sister was also very sad in the beginning.
But she always said, why did my parents-- one of her sayings.
Why did my parents have to end like this?
Why didn't they take me?
I'm a poor girl.
I cannot see very well.
So my job was to lift her spirits.
Or actually, to scream at her.
To tell her to stop, you know, acting like she did.
This was the circumstance-- these were the circumstances that we were in, and we better make the best of it.
And we did.
So you were tough in front of her, but you--
I was very, very tough in front of her.
Yeah.
I didn't know I was, but I was.
I was.
I was not aware at all that anything was wrong with me.
Tima and Ali-- Tima-- the gentleman who had, you know, saved us, and brought us, and did all these dangerous things for us-- was a part of the underground.
And he did unbelievable things, of which I will name a few of them.
He was-- among-- he had-- was hiding-- I do not know exactly, but I would say 15 Jewish people in a forest.
Hidden in the forest, in a-- they had dug a hole underneath trees.
And I think they were-- I do not know exactly, but I think they were about 15.
They had-- yes.
And Tima had then, of course, his friends in the underground who would go there twice or three times per week to bring food to these people.
And you can just really imagine how dangerous that was.
They did not-- one of the people who he also rescued was an American [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] airman who had to been in hiding with my brother [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for about two weeks.
And Tima went to where my brother was and took his bicycle and another bicycle that we bought some place.
And Albert Stern is the name of the American navigator.
He was a navigator, he was not a pilot.
And he then was transported the same way that we were transported originally, originally.
Only that was much more dangerous.
I mean, we were only Jews, and the Jews would have just been, you know, ended.
But he was an American pilot, navigator, who was being rescued by Dutch underground people.
Also about 25 miles, the same-- you know.
But they had-- they had given Albert Stern a passport that he was deaf and dumb and that he was Dutch.
Albert Stern, he was something like-- oh, maybe, I'm not sure-- 6' 4'.
I had never seen anybody as tall.
And he was, one night, in this same bunker where all the other Jews were.
In the same town where we were.
Two days or three days later, one of Tima's underground people was picked up by the Germans.
That meant, of course, enormous, enormous different things for us also.
The danger that this underground person would say something, and say that we were in Tima's-- let's say, in Tima's fiance's house was, of course, very, very normal.
So the first thing was to bring us out of the danger that we were in.
We had to flee the house that we had been in for a year.
We had to go someplace else.
And by then it was January, '44?
Yes.
By then it was January of 1944.
The night before we were transported-- so that meant this whole year from February to the next January, we had not been out of the house.
Not been outside.
In hiding.
Not hardly walked.
Only sometimes when we thought it was a very, very safe that evening, we-- I would sometimes run up and down the steps, the stairs, to have some exercise.
Normally speaking, we would sit in bed all day with the sewing machine in front of me.
And then at night, we would take the sewing machine away, and I would get to sleep in the same bed.
That--
She read.
Oh, we knitted.
We knitted.
Knitting she could do because I could help her with the knitting part.
If she dropped one of the stitches, she would hand it to me, and I would, you know, I would pick up the stitch.
Yes.
But mainly Ali, who had been in the same shop that I had been in.
And at the time there were not too many people who could find people to repair for them.
I would help Ali to do her repair work, and Ali did-- we-- that was in the beginning, I think, of nylons.
And she had a special needle.
Somebody who had a runner in the stockings, we-- we sometimes would repair nylon stockings.
But I would help and repair sewing.
I was very happy, even if I didn't like my mother to teach me how to sew.
But it helped me a great deal through a lot of things.
And this was one of them.
Yes.
OK.
No.
Such religious people, such beautiful people.
As far-- you know, if you go and they-- the girls, the rest of the family, everybody was-- there were only two brothers, let's say.
There were two brothers and five sisters.
OK.
So there was just-- everybody knew each other.
And I-- we had-- and I had known them all already before the war.
I had been there many, many times and visited there.
And they had visited our house.
They knew my parents, also.
They had known my parents.
So for me, that was such a good thing.
So when I talked about somebody, they knew what I was talking about.
Very, very helpful.
I felt safe and at home.
And sometimes, their priest would come and visit.
He knew that we were there.
He knew.
He would come to visit, and just come to talk to us.
Nothing in particular, but just trying to enhance our spirits.
Religion was not one of the things.
He knew that we were Jewish.
And as I said before, Jewish was something that we had at home.
But I-- I was very familiar with the Catholic religion because it was surrounding me all these years with everybody that I knew of.
You know, so there was no problem there at all.
I would like to tell you the night that we-- the night before we fled, if I could.
We were brought down to the basement.
And in the basement, they-- in the basement, they had put in a kind of-- whichever.
They had made a like-- I-- I do not know how I should say this now.
OK.
They had-- their basement had a wall like this, and they had brought out a tiny little part of the basement further into the front.
And a very little hole in the bottom.
We crawled through the hole.
Stood up all night.
The only air that we got was through a-- through nothing, actually.
A little hole in the bottom.
And we were afraid that we would die because we couldn't breathe.
But we made it.
